summary: Map the terrain for adding next: SuggestedAction[] to all tool results in .opencode/tools/*.ts

execution_pipeline_summary.flow: AI SDK streamText → tools.ts resolve() → for each registry tool → toolSemaphore.withPermit → PhaseGate check → plugin.trigger('tool.execute.before') → TypedResult.wrapExecute(toolID, item.execute(args, ctx)) → plugin.trigger('tool.execute.after') → result returned to AI SDK
execution_pipeline_summary.key_insight: ALL built-in tools registered via ToolRegistry go through TypedResult.wrapExecute() at line 108, which already adds suggestedNextTools + nextAffordances via formatOutput(). The question is whether .opencode/tools/*.ts plugin tools ALSO go through this path (if they're registered via the Plugin→Registry bridge) or bypass it entirely (if Plugin has its own execution pipeline).

smoking_guns.title: Infrastructure already has NextAffordance — plugin tools don't use it
smoking_guns.detail: packages/opencode/src/tool/typed-result.ts defines NextAffordance interface, NextAffordanceSchema, affordancesAfterSuccess(), affordancesFor(), formatOutput(), and wrapExecute() — all with full affordance plumbing. However, the .opencode/tools/*.ts plugin tools bypass this infrastructure entirely, returning raw JSON.stringify({...}) strings. The affordance system only applies to the Effect-TS tool layer (native tools in packages/opencode/src/tool/), not the plugin layer.

smoking_guns.title: Ad-hoc affordance fields already exist but are inconsistent
smoking_guns.detail: smart_delegate.ts returns both 'next' (string) and 'hint' (string) fields. fragment.ts has 'hint'. feedback.ts has conditional 'warning' suggesting specific tool calls. roadmap.ts has 'recommendation'. These are ad-hoc, informally specified, and inconsistent — some tools have them, most don't. They prove the concept but need formalization.

smoking_guns.title: Two parallel tool systems with different affordance levels
smoking_guns.detail: The repo has TWO tool systems: (1) Effect-TS native tools in packages/opencode/src/tool/ with full TypedToolResult + NextAffordance wrapping, and (2) Plugin tools in .opencode/tools/*.ts using @opencode-ai/plugin which return raw JSON strings. The affordance gap means plugin tools provide zero structured next-action guidance despite being the primary tool set. The question isn't 'should we add affordances?' — it's 'how do we bridge the plugin layer to the already-existing affordance infrastructure?'
files: node_modules/@opencode-ai/plugin/src/tool.ts:36-44 (ToolResult type — supports metadata but no affordance field)

sdk_interface.tool_signature: tool<Args extends z.ZodRawShape>({ description, args, execute(args, context): Promise<ToolResult> })
sdk_interface.ToolResult: string | { title?: string, output: string, metadata?: { [key: string]: any }, attachments?: ToolAttachment[] }
sdk_interface.ToolContext: { sessionID, messageID, agent, directory, worktree, abort: AbortSignal, metadata(input), ask(input) }
sdk_interface.note: ToolResult.metadata is the natural insertion point for affordances — it's already typed as { [key: string]: any }

key_functions.toolCategory: line 235 — maps tool IDs to categories: read/write/search/execute/manage/plan/diagnose
key_functions.suggestedToolsAfterSuccess: line 245 — returns string[] of follow-up tools
key_functions.suggestedToolsFor: line 251 — context-aware suggestions based on status + errorKind
key_functions.affordancesAfterSuccess: line 269 — returns NextAffordance[] based on tool category
key_functions.formatOutput: line 353 — formats TypedToolResult into LLM-readable string with affordance lines
key_functions.wrapExecute: line 425 — wraps native tool execution, auto-attaches affordances to metadata

approach_options.name: Option A: Add next to ToolResult.metadata
approach_options.description: Return the structured form of ToolResult ({ output, metadata }) instead of raw JSON strings. Put `next: SuggestedAction[]` in metadata. This uses the existing SDK type without changes.
pros: metadata is already typed as Record<string, any>
cons: Requires changing every tool's return format from string to object
Breaks any code that expects raw string output

approach_options.name: Option B: Embed next in JSON string output
approach_options.description: Add a `next` field to the JSON objects that tools already stringify. Keep the string return type.
pros: Minimal change — just add one field
cons: LLM must parse JSON to see suggestions
Not structured in the SDK type system

approach_options.name: Option C: Bridge plugin tools to the Effect-TS affordance system
approach_options.description: Wrap plugin tool execution in the existing wrapExecute() from typed-result.ts, adding TypedToolResult affordances automatically. Plugin tools don't need to change — the wrapper adds affordances based on tool ID matching the existing toolCategory() function.
pros: toolCategory() already maps most plugin tool IDs to categories
cons: Requires changing the execution pipeline where plugin tools are called
Needs new toolCategory entries for plugin-specific tools (fragment, gate, coordinate, etc.)

approach_options.name: Option D: Hybrid — B for plugin tools + extend toolCategory for bridging
approach_options.description: Add `next: SuggestedAction[]` as a JSON field in plugin tool results (Option B) AND extend toolCategory() to recognize all plugin tool IDs so wrapExecute() can also work if the pipeline changes later.
pros: Plugin tools have explicit, per-action affordances (better than generic category-based)
cons: Two affordance systems exist side-by-side until unified

design_recommendations.pipeline_critical_question: Do .opencode/tools/*.ts plugin tools go through TypedResult.wrapExecute() at tools.ts:108? If yes, they already get category-based affordances (toolCategory() string-matching DOES match plugin tool IDs like 'smart_edit', 'smart_grep'). If no, they need a separate affordance injection point. This is the FIRST question the architect must answer.
design_recommendations.recommendation_rationale: Option B is the lowest-risk, immediately-deployable approach that matches existing patterns. Adding `next` to the JSON output is purely additive — no existing code breaks. Simultaneously extending toolCategory() in typed-result.ts to cover all 31 plugin tool IDs makes the infrastructure layer ready for future unification. The SuggestedAction type should match NextAffordance: { action: string, target?: string, command?: string, when?: string, args?: Record<string, any>, hint: string }.

fields.action: string — tool name to call next (e.g., 'smart_edit', 'smart_bun')
fields.args: Record<string, any> (optional) — recommended arguments for the next call
fields.when: string — condition describing when this action is appropriate

out_of_scope_findings.summary: Two tools in registry are placeholders (delegate.ts, session-diff.ts) — erodes trust in tool registry
ToolCache.getOrCompute is defined but never called — dead API surface
ToolCache.inflight map mutated via direct reference mutation bypassing Ref.update
Bidirectional dependency: tool/registry.ts ↔ session/{todo,instruction} — fragile init order
github.ts is a stub returning 'not_implemented' — provides zero value
toolCategory() in typed-result.ts uses brittle string-matching (toolID.includes('read')) — doesn't match plugin tool IDs like 'read_source', 'smart_edit', 'smart_write'
